purpose: Decomposes above-target inflation into structural (policy-resistant) and cyclical (policy-responsive) components to determine appropriate policy response
components: Structural components: housing supply deficit (10-20bp), deglobalization/tariffs (10-20bp), healthcare/insurance (10-15bp), energy transition (5-15bp)
Cyclical components: residual labor tightness, fiscal impulse, post-pandemic demand normalization
Agent estimates: rates_strategist_02 at 60-70% structural, macro_strategist_01 at 30-40% structural, generalist_02 at ~40% structural
current_assessment: Estimates range from 30-70% structural — the two most methodologically rigorous agents reach nearly opposite conclusions. The divergence stems from classification choices: housing supply deficit as structural vs. labor tightness as cyclical. This is the highest-priority question for future research as it determines whether the inflation regime is amenable to conventional policy or requires structural reform.
limitations: Wide confidence intervals (+/-20-30bp on each component)
Classification of factors as structural vs. cyclical is itself debatable
Different methodologies yield materially different results
Overconfidence in point estimates is the primary epistemic risk
